speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you spend in Lublin?

speaker: interviewee
text: How long was I in the orphanage in Lublin? I think I was in the orphanage about a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your sisters and brother there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Since '45-- yeah, till about '46. I was about a year in the orphanage.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: And my brother and sisters were in the orphanage with me, too. Eda and my brother Moisha-- Mark-- were in the orphanage with me. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that entire year you spent--

speaker: interviewee
text: In an orphanage.

speaker: interviewer
text: Looking for family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we always looked for family. We always look for family. Always looked. They had this headquarters, and everybody always went to look for the list. Because whoever survived would write down their name, and that-- this was-- this went on for a long, long time. I don't think-- well, finally after many, many years, you finally give up. Because you were hoping maybe somebody survive. Because, even though we knew that my mother and sister were taken to Treblinka, and my aunts, and uncles, and everybody. But somehow, you know, you always have hope. Maybe, maybe somebody survived. Maybe somewhere we'll find someone.

speaker: interviewer
text: And for many, many years, after I had gone and seen movies, you know, about the Holocaust or whatever, you always would look and see. Maybe we'll recognize a face. Maybe. But after a certain amount of years, you give up, you know? They're not here anymore. They're gone. Where did you go from the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my-- my sisters were in Poland. My two sisters had married. And they came to the orphanage to tell us that they're dispersing the orphanage. Because there were also a lot of non-Jewish children in that orphanage, too. And they were taking us someplace else. Now, meanwhile, I had found out, while I was in the orphanage, there was a little girl who had survived the war too, but she was hidden with Polish people. And her mother had survived a war in a camp. Her mother had found out that this little girl is in that orphanage in Poland, in Lublin, came looking for this little girl. But meanwhile, my sister, Eda, and my brother had been already taken out of the orphanage. They were going to West Germany. They were taking him to the American side, and they left me behind. But my other sister, my married sisters, were still in Lublin. And they had said to me they didn't want me to go, you know, with the-- with the ca-- with the orphanage to Germany, because I was alone. But meanwhile, they too were thinking of leaving Poland, because Poland was not the place to be. It was a hateful country. They were not very nice to us, and we just didn't want to live there anymore. It was-- you know, you were a person without a country. Poland really was not your country. You had nowhere to go. So they all decided to go to Germany, with the American, on the West-- with the Americans. And finally, they came to take the whole orphanage, and I decided to go with them. And I went where my sister and my brother were, to the-- in Germany. But in order for me to go there, they couldn't take the whole orphanage out, so they had to individually take you. So when this woman came looking at the orphanage for her little girl, my sisters had asked this woman to please bring me with.

speaker: interviewer
text: She says, please, she's my little sister. Why don't you take her with? So the woman said to them, OK. If it's not going to be too difficult for you, we will take her with. Well, we couldn't just cross any old way the borders, you know? We had to cross Czechoslovakia and go into Germany. We had to go at night.

speaker: interviewee
text: While the Ger-- while the Polish people were on the borders, you couldn't cross. We were afraid. So at night, we would smuggle through the border. She, the mother with her child, and with me. And we were terribly, terribly afraid. Because if they would have caught us at night, too, they would have sent us right back. I don't know at that point if they would have killed us. I don't know. Because it was already, you know, after the war. But they certainly would have kept us in Poland. They would not have allowed us to go back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we smuggled again. We smuggled through the Polish border, and we came to Czechoslovakia. Then we got through that border. And finally, we came to Germany. And that's when we found my sister, Eda, and my brother Mark.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then later, eventually, my two sisters came out of Poland too. They all-- we all left Poland. We did not want to live there anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany, we went to a camp by the name of Landsberg. And this was also a displaced person camp. We were there for awhile. And then, the organized-- the Jewish community organized itself. They had a committee. And Israelis and European Jews who had survived, they were trying to organize all the young people. And they decided to take us to another camp in Feldafing, which was not far from Landsberg, in Munich, Germany. And they organized like a kibbutz-- the Israeli style kibbutz style, where they had children from all over Europe. They were a mixture of young ones, old ones. I really was one of the youngest. There was one-- a few other children, younger ones than I was. But they were the ones who survived while hidden by the Polish people. They were, like, maybe 10-year-olds, 12-year-olds, older and younger. It was a mixture of different European children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the kibbutz sort of became like a big family, you know? Because we all had each other to count on. Everybody had lost their parents. Everybody had lost a brother or a sister, grandparents. And the kibbutz became part of the family. This was our family.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was in Feldafing for almost-- well, a half a year. At least a half a year, till 1946. And then, Israel-- a lot of people started going to Israel with illegal Aliyahs. And finally, they dispersed the kibbutz. A lot of people went. They couldn't take the whole kibbutz. My brother was one of the lucky ones. But he went with the illegal Aliyah, with the illegal trans-- transport. And we were hoping to follow him. But eventually, of course, we didn't. And Feldafing was dispersed. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then we went back to another displaced person camp, Neu Freimann, which was near Munich, Germany. And from there-- I lived there for about three months or so, or maybe a little bit longer. My two sisters, my married sisters, also came there to live. And they organized schools. The Jewish community organized schools. And I started going to school there. And life started becoming a little bit of a normal-- normal way, if you can call in a living a displaced person camp normal. And I started going to school. But I lived with my oldest sister at the time. The Germans had taken out some of their German citizens and given us the homes. They were small, little bungalows, and several families had to share one little bungalow. And I lived with my sister. My sister, Eda, and I lived with my two married sisters. At that time, they were already married. And life-- like I said, you try to make a-- as normal as you possibly can under these circumstances.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you decide to come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had heard a lot about the Golden Medina, the beautiful country of America. I heard it from my father as a little girl. I had also heard it about-- from my uncle, the one that ended in Russian and eventually did immigrate to America. I had heard beautiful things, you know, that you find gold on the streets. Which, of course, is not true. But one day I was sitting in school, and American soldier came in from the [INAUDIBLE]. And he had asked if there were orphans, and if they wanted to go to America. And I happened to have raised my hand, because I was an orphan.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said, well, do you want to go to America? And I said, well, I really would have to ask my older sister, you know, if I should go or not. So that day, I came home from school, from this makeshift school where they had all different kinds of ages, not just my age. And I told my sister, Hannah. I said, you know, that a soldier came in today, and he wanted to know if I want to go to America, that all the orphans can go. And what do you think I should do? Well, right away, of course, my sister being that she was already the oldest and married, and she said, absolutely.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's wonderful. It would be a wonderful opportunity for you. You know, you can get an education. It'll be a new life for you completely. But, she says, you cannot go unless your sister, Eda, goes. I will not let you go alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the following day, I went back. And I told him that I would be interested in going to America, but my sister, Edam which at that time was only 17, would have to come with me. And they said fine, being that she was still underage, you know, a minor. And I signed up. And I said, we're going to America.

speaker: interviewee
text: And within a few months-- I think it took about three months-- we were notified that we would have to be-- go to the German consulate. And you have to go through a physical, you know, a medical exam and all of that. And that was it. This was simple as that. That's how I came to America. But my uncle-- my uncle at that time encouraged us too, definitely. He said, you have to go there. It's a wonderful country. And the reason he was so strong about it, because his wife had relatives in Texas. And they were also going to America. My uncle did go to America with his wife. And therefore, I-- I came here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, he didn't come here directly to Minneapolis.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. I came to New York. We were in New York for about a week. We came to Brunnenhof, and we went by ship. And that, too, was a terrible time for me. I was six. The complete nine days I was very ill. But I knew where I was going. And we arrived in New York. And needless to say-- we arrived at night, actually, in the evening. And I remember there were people waiting for us at the port. And they had hot milk for us, and some doughnuts, and bismarcks, and all these goodies. And I'm still complaining till today about my sweet tooth. I said the Americans are to blame, because they-- they are the ones who introduced me to doughnuts and cocoa and milk. But of course, being that I was so ill, I couldn't eat any of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took us right away on buses. There were 21 children on that-- on that group with us. And what can I tell you? Driving through the city of New York-- the lights, the people. I mean, it was like being in a dream. It was just absolutely unbelievable.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we finally arrived to-- to Brooklyn, and they had a home where all the newcomers, or refugees-- it was sort of a stepping stone, like. This is where you came, and from that place, they dispersed you to different parts of the country.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were there for about a week. They took us-- I remember going to a doctor's office. They examined us, and we checked out OK. We were pretty healthy at that point, because we had lived in the DP camp already, and under the American supervision, things were very good for us. And then they took us to the Statue of Liberty. They took us all over to see the-- in New York, the-- what's the big building? The-- not Foshay Tower, that's Minneapolis. What is the-- Empire State Building?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Empire State Building in New York. And they just gave us a beautiful tour. It was just wonderful. They took us to wonderful restaurants. And then, from then on, they sent us to Chicago. May 7, 1947-- no, that's the date I arrived to Minneapolis. It was the end of April that they send us to Chicago to an orphanage. They found an orphanage in Chicago. And in Chicago, we were in the orphanage for about six weeks. And it was an American orphanage. They had American children, too, at that home. It was called the Marks Nathan Hall Home for Children. And the orphanage was-- it was good. I mean, it was a good home. The people were very, very kind to us. It was a little bit strange for us, because we were among American children. We did not speak the language. We couldn't communicate very well. I remember we went by train from New York to Chicago. They sent us by train. We did not speak English. And I remember it was an overnight train. And the following morning, when we got up, we noticed that our shoes, our old shoes, had been polished. And we couldn't figure out how-- you know. And they-- later on, of course, we found out that that's what they did on these trains, the overnights. But the only bad part on that train was that we could not communicate. We couldn't order any food. We couldn't ask for anything. So we drank a lot of water. That was-- that was the fun part.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were three of us, from New York going to Chicago. And then, when we were in Chicago, like I said for six weeks. And then they found a foster home for me in Minneapolis.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know anything about Minneapolis before you came here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had not heard a thing. Never heard anything. Never heard anything about Minneapolis. Not at all. But while we were in Chicago, too, the people treated us very nicely. They were very kind to us. They took us around the tours. They gave us tours to see the city. And I started school right away. Because they-- it was in April, and school was still going on. So they wanted me to start school right away. So I remember they took me to the school. And of course, didn't speak. All I spoke was Yiddish and Hebrew. And they put me into a-- either it was the first grade or the kindergarten. And I came into the class, and I remember the teacher asking any of the children if they spoke Yiddish, because that's the only language I spoke. And Hebrew, I spoke, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one little girl got up, and she said she spoke Yiddish. So here I was thrilled. And the only thing she said to me in Yiddish was [YIDDISH], which means 'go to sleep.' And I sort of was startled, and I started to laugh. I said, why is she telling me to go to sleep? But of course, I realized that she did not speak Yiddish, and I could not speak English.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went to school till May, till about May 6th or so, till they were ready to send me off to Minneapolis. And again, we went by train. But of course this train was a little bit shorter, and at that point we could already understand a little bit English. We could ask something or other. And I came to Minneapolis July 2nd, 1947. I was in-- in Chicago till then. And they found a foster home for me, and my sister, Eda, was put in a boarding house. They separated us, which was not very advisable. Now-- now they realize. Later on, actually, they realized that it was not very smart to have separated us. And-- but the woman that took me, the foster home that took me in, really did not have room for another child. They just wanted-- they just wanted one-- one child.

speaker: interviewer
text: So now, it is-- you're about 15 years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was already 14 and 1/2. Yeah, almost 15. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're living in a foster home in Minneapolis--

speaker: interviewee
text: I live in a foster home in Minneapolis. And the funny thing is, while I was in Chicago in the orphanage, people used to say that Minneapolis was a big farm. They said, you're going to a big farm. They told me it was very cold. Well, the cold didn't bother me. I was used to the cold weather. And somehow or another, I believe that this is probably why they sent me here altogether, because they probably thought that I would-- you know, weather-wise, that I would not have as many problems. Somehow or another, I wish maybe they had sent me to Florida or California. But it's OK. I'm happy being here. So they found this foster home for me, and I started going to school. They sent me to school-- to public school. And I lived there for a few years, till I got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: How hard was it to explain to a foster family where you had been, what you had gone through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was very, very hard. First of all, communicating-wise, because the foster parents did not speak Yiddish. They had a hard time with Yiddish, and I did not speak English very well. But lucky enough for me, they had a father, an old man that lived with them-- the grandfather-- who spoke Yiddish. So I was able to communicate with him-- through him, to speak to the foster parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was very, very difficult. It was a very difficult period for me because, first of all, they did not have a room for me. They had a room where a bed had to be set up for me every night, and every morning, the bed had to be taken away. So I did not, again, have this permanence. You know what I mean? I did not have a place where I could really call it my own.

speaker: interviewee
text: These people were extremely kind and wonderful that they took in this foster child, because they really did not know who I was and what I was. They had heard a lot of stories that we are like animals. You know, people-- you're doing-- you don't know what you're doing. Her name was Julia Lewis. They said, you really don't know what you're undertaking, taking in this child that has been in a war for five years. She might be an animal, for all you know. So it was a little difficult, but they treated me very nicely. They were very nice, and I was also very nice to them. I did a lot of house chores. You know, I helped them at the home. And I went to school. And the only part that I could remember was this idea of not having a room of my own-- a permanent place where I could really call my own.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you got married.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I got married. Well, I finished school first. I finished school. I went to Hebrew school. I graduated that school. And my life became a little bit of a normal life for a teenager. Even though I was way beyond my years, because I had survived a war, I had lost my mother and father, I had lost a beautiful family. But everybody tried to make it normal for me. I had a wonderful social worker who really tried very, very hard to make me happy. But life was very, very cruel, too. Because, you know, when you're in a school with young children, you don't speak the language, children have a tendency sometimes to be very cruel, because they themselves cannot understand.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were many, many times where I would just sit at school and not say anything at all. For a whole year, I remember not opening my mouth, because I was embarrassed. Because if I would say something, the children would laugh.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, you know, when you get laughter, you don't talk. You just decide, oh, I don't want them to be laughing at me. So I didn't talk. But eventually, I got used to it. I got used to it. And I learned the language, and I skipped a couple grades, so I was able to catch up with my age. Because when I went into the seventh grade, I was almost 15 years old, and most of the children in seventh grade were like 12 and 13-year-old. So it was a little more difficult. So I skipped a couple grades, and I caught up with myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after you were married, there are-- how many children do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have three children. We have a son, Phillip Marshall, we have a daughter, Pamela Joy, and we have a son, Gary Jay. Wonderful kids. Beautiful children that I'm very, very proud of.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then there are some grandchildren, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we have eight grandchildren. We have a grandson, Jesse, our oldest. Then we have a grandson, Adam. Those are my chil-- my son's children. Then we have a grandson, Jonathan. Then my daughter has four children. She has a grand-- a daughter, Sarah, a grandson, Gabriel, a grandson, Aaron, and a granddaughter, Rebecca. Then my youngest son has a little granddaughter-- my granddaughter, his daughter-- Raleigh. Raleigh Sarah is her name. I have beautiful grandchildren that I'm very, very proud of, and children that I'm extremely proud of.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there are some things you seem to have brought from your hometown. You-- there are celebrations in this house.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, thank God that I was able to continue with all the celebrations. I continue with the holidays. I'm very, very fortunate. I have a wonderful husband who's also a Holocaust survivor. He survived the Dachau concentration camp. He has worked very, very hard. He came here with nothing, and he worked very hard. And I'm very, very fortunate. He's a wonderful, wonderful man, and a wonderful father. And-- and my kids all went into the field of helping humanities. After I had told my children-- from the day that they were very, very young, I was also very fortunate that I was able to talk to them. I know a lot of Holocaust survivors had a terrible time. They could not open up and tell things to their children. They were overprotective. They were-- they were just afraid for them. But I was very, very lucky. Maybe it is because I survived when I was young. And I-- in a certain way, I had a little bit of a normal life growing up in America. And-- but when I used to tell the stories to my children, I remember when my son first started talking about what he wanted to do. He decided to become a doctor. He wanted to help humanity. My daughter became a nurse, also wanting to help humanity. My youngest son became businessman. He worked with his father for many, many years. And I am very proud of them, because they're wonderful citizens. They are wonderful, worthwhile American citizens.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's wonderful. And your mother used to sing you some songs when you were a child that you sing to your children and to your grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there any one that's special?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember a song my mother used to sing when I was a little girl. She sang that song from the First World War. She said she was a young girl during the First World War. And I remember singing that song once to my grandson, Jesse, my oldest grandson. And I started to cry.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said, Grandma, why are you crying? And I explained him the story while I was crying. I said, I'm crying, because I remember the song my mother used to sing to me. And of course, Jesse, you know my mother died in the Holocaust. And at that time, Jesse was only a little boy, and I didn't want to go into details, because he would not have understood what I was saying anyway.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that song has always-- I've always sung that song too. And I sing it a lot. Even I sing it sometimes when I feel sad, or I feel something bothers me. And I just sing that song. And that song makes me feel warm. Because when I think that song, I remember my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you sing it? You have such a beautiful voice.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll just sing a few-- just one verse, because it's a rather long-- long song. [SINGING IN YIDDISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a song about a soldier who goes out to war, and he gets killed in the war. And a black-- not a pigeon. What are those? A [NON-ENGLISH] is a black-- it's a bird that comes and rests on his grave. And he-- the person i the grave is asking this bird.

speaker: interviewee
text: I will give you my hands, please give me your eyes. And when you fly away, fly to my mother, but do not tell her about my death. For if you tell my mother about my death, she will cry all the time. Just don't tell her the sad story. And of course, it's a very, very long song. It tells her about, how long is this war going to last, and how much blood has to be shed in order for this war to go on. It's a very, very sad song. And I sing that song all the time. Now I have a seven-month-old little granddaughter, and I sing that song to her too, hoping that they will all remember some of the Jewish songs that I taught them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is-- is there any special reason why it was important for you to do a testimony?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it's very important. Like I said, there had to be a reason why I survived. Why did they choose me? Why Reva Mandelbaum Kibort had to survive? A young girl, where 150 million children died. Why was I selected to live? I mean, sometimes, you know, your mind is just wondering, why me? But I think there had to be a purpose. Because if there-- if I were not here, and if my husband was not here, and maybe some of the other survivors were not here, nobody would have known about this. Maybe this thing would have gone on another time. Another Holocaust would've existed. People have to be told. Hopefully, we hope that, by the things that happened to us, other-- another Holocaust will never happen again. But it is absolutely mind-boggling that anything like this could have happened in the 20th century. Such meanness. Such, like, barbarians, you know, to-- to have done this to people. And for no reason, for no cause. We just hope and pray that people will learn that, just because somebody dresses differently, or looks differently, or speaks differently, or believes in something else, that they're just as human as everybody else. You do not go out and hate him for that. You just do not do it. You have to be tolerant of somebody else.

speaker: interviewer
text: We are all here for one reason, you know? We all have the same feelings, the same blood, the same love, the same-- we all want the same thing for our children, for our families. And I feel that it's very important for me to be here and tell you this story, not only for my children. Because I hope and pray that my children will tell this story to their children and to their children, to generations to come.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I just feel that it's very, very important for me to tell the story. That someone cannot come along and say, the-- the Holocaust never existed. They cannot say that it was nothing but a hoax that the Jewish people create it. It's just-- it's not so. It is there. I am an orphan. I was deprived of a normal life. I was deprived, as a child, from a mother and father. My mother and father never saw me grow up. They cannot-- they cannot see the pleasures that I have now. And I resent it very, very much. I resent what they did to me, that they cannot see my children and my grandchildren. This is what I import-- it's important to tell us the story. Hopefully, this will never, never happen again. But we all have to prepare. We can never repeat-- the thing can never be repeated again. Ever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
